turns.text: Hello Corie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you wanting to listen. Honestly, it’s been a bit of a lonely road lately. Living out here in the woods, it’s quiet—almost too quiet when you’re spending your mornings staring at LH strips and trying to make sense of your own data. 

I’m twenty-eight, and I already have three kids, so I thought I knew exactly how this worked. But this time feels different. My body just isn’t doing what it used to, and I’ve been tracking everything—basal temps, cycles, the works—just trying to catch a sign that things are moving in the right direction. 

When I went to see my doctor recently, I walked in with my notes and some questions about follicular development. I felt like I’d done my homework, you know? But she just gave me that... that condescending smile. She told me I’m "low risk" and to just "keep trying." It felt like she didn't even look at the charts I brought. I left feeling like a nuisance rather than a patient. I know I’m healthy on paper, but I’m the one living in this body every day, and I know when something feels off. Being so far from a specialist just adds this heavy, logistical layer of anxiety to everything. I just want someone to take me seriously.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I expected a partnership. When I started this, I pictured sitting down with a doctor who would look at my cycles, validate the effort I’m putting in, and maybe say, "Okay, Corie, if we don't see a change in a couple of months, here’s a plan." I thought we’d be a team working toward the same goal. I assumed that because I’m proactive and have done the research, the medical side would be even more engaged.

But the reality? It’s been nothing like that. It feels more like I’m standing outside a locked gate. My doctor seems to view my involvement as... I don’t know, obsessive? Like I’m just a young woman who needs to go home, relax, and stop thinking so much. When I tried to bring up induction or even just get her to look at the patterns I’ve identified, she treated it like I was questioning her authority instead of advocating for my own body. 

It’s isolating. I’m doing all this work at home—the temperature checks, the constant monitoring—and when I finally make that long, exhausting drive into town for an appointment, I’m met with a wall of "wait and see." It makes me feel like I’m failing, and the fact that I have to drive so far just to feel unheard makes it all feel that much more impossible.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise, and honestly the most painful one, was realizing that my medical history—having three successful pregnancies—is actually being used against me. I thought that track record would mean the doctor would trust me when I said, "Something is different this time." Instead, it’s become my primary barrier. Because I’m "low risk" and have three healthy kids, she seems to think I have no reason to be concerned, almost like my fertility is a faucet she can just turn on and off without any effort.

I went into that office expecting someone to listen to my observations about my cycle. I thought, *They’re the experts, they’ll see what I’m seeing.* But the disconnect was shocking. I was essentially dismissed as a nervous mom-to-be who just needs to "stop tracking so much." 

It’s a strange, hollow feeling to realize that my deep, analytical approach to my own health is viewed as a character flaw rather than an asset. I wasn't prepared for the feeling of being treated like a number on a chart rather than a person struggling with a very real, very quiet ache. I didn't expect to feel so alone in a room with a medical professional.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I expected to feel empowered. Before we started trying for our fourth, I felt really secure—I’ve done this three times, I know my body, and I felt like I was entering this phase with a mature, steady mindset. I imagined myself being this calm, "go-with-the-flow" mother who would just welcome the process as it came.

But the reality? It’s not calm at all. It’s become this all-consuming, high-stakes project. When the month passes and I see that negative test, it’s not just a "try again next time" moment; it feels like a personal rejection from my own body. My emotional state is so much more fragile than I anticipated. I find myself spiraling into these quiet, dark places in the bathroom, staring at those little plastic strips until my eyes ache, wondering why the spark isn't catching. 

I’m also carrying this guilt. I have three beautiful children, and I feel like I shouldn't be allowed to feel this hollow, but I do. The isolation of being out here in the woods makes those feelings echo louder. I’m not the relaxed mother I thought I’d be; I’m a woman who feels like she’s losing control, and every day that passes without a positive result makes that lack of control feel more permanent.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

The biggest thing missing is someone who acknowledges that "low risk" doesn't mean "no pain." I need someone—a professional—to look me in the eye and say, "I see that you’re struggling, and your feelings are valid," instead of just shrugging off my concerns as anxiety. When the doctor stays so detached, it makes me feel like my intuition is invalid, and that's a lonely place to be.

With my husband, it’s complicated. He’s wonderful, he really is, but he’s practical. He sees our three healthy kids and he’s content, so he doesn't understand why I’m obsessing over hormone levels or why the negative tests hurt so much. He tries to tell me to "just relax," which I know is meant to be comforting, but it just makes me feel more misunderstood. I can’t explain to him that I’m not just trying to get pregnant—I’m trying to reclaim a piece of myself that feels like it’s slipping away.

And my wider network? I don't really have one out here. My neighbors are wonderful, but this isn't the kind of thing you bring up over the fence. I’m craving a space where I don’t have to justify my desire for another child or defend why I’m so fixated on my health. I just need to be heard without being told to "calm down."
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find clear, actionable answers. I thought if I used the right apps and read the right forums, I’d find a roadmap—like, "if X happens, then do Y." I wanted a logical, structured way to navigate this, thinking that if I just had the right data, I could master the outcome.

What I actually found was a mess of contradictory advice and commercialized "wellness" content that feels more like marketing than medicine. Everything online is either way too basic—"just drink water and relax!"—or it’s buried in these high-level medical forums that make me feel like I need a PhD to understand my own progesterone levels. It’s overwhelming. 

Instead of clarity, I found an echo chamber of other women who are just as frustrated and dismissed as I am. It wasn't what I needed. I didn't need more algorithms or generalized blog posts; I needed a bridge to actual medical expertise. The apps tell me when I’m ovulating, sure, but they don't help when the biology doesn't align with the screen. They give you the "when," but they leave you entirely alone with the "why." It just ends up fueling my obsession, giving me more data to analyze while I sit out here in the woods, waiting for someone with a white coat to actually care about what all these numbers mean.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The gap is basically a canyon. The digital world—the apps, the fertility tracking communities, the deeper research I’ve done—encourages me to be an investigator of my own body. They teach me that if my luteal phase is short or my temperatures are erratic, it’s a data point worth looking into. There’s a whole language of reproductive health out there that treats me like an active participant in my own cycle.

Then I take that data to my provider, and it’s like I’ve walked into a completely different universe. She acts like the things I’ve learned—the very things that help me understand my cycle—are basically irrelevant noise. The digital world says, "Here is a problem to solve," while she says, "There is no problem to see." 

That gap is exactly where my frustration lives. The internet makes me feel like I’m empowered to advocate for myself, but my doctor makes me feel like my advocacy is actually a symptom of being "high-strung." It’s gaslighting, really. I’m left trying to reconcile the clinical, "wait-and-see" approach of a doctor who hasn't seen me in six months with the constant, daily reality of my own charts. It feels like I'm reading two different books, and only one of them is actually about *me*.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic illness in the traditional sense, but in my mind, this *is* a condition. This feeling—that my body is out of sync and failing to respond—feels just as real and just as urgent as any diagnosis. 

When I went into that appointment, I expected a "pre-conception plan." I wanted to hear things like, "Let’s run a basic hormone panel just to rule out any thyroid issues" or "Let’s look at your follicular development to ensure things are firing correctly." I expected them to treat the *pre-conception* phase with as much medical rigor as they do the actual pregnancy. I thought the healthcare system would see my desire for a fourth child as a project we could manage together, starting with a baseline assessment.

Instead, when I sought that help, I was essentially told to come back only after I’d failed to conceive for a year or more. The response was complete passivity. It was like saying, "We don’t care if your body is struggling now; we only care once you’ve officially hit the threshold of a 'problem.'" It makes me feel like I’m being forced to let my health deteriorate or to waste months of my life, all so I can fit into some insurance-defined box of "infertility." It’s demoralizing to be told that until I’m broken enough, I’m not worth their time.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

The system has never once reached out to me. Not once. It’s been entirely on me to drag the conversation into the light. I’m the one who has to track the cycles, print out the charts, and then mentally prepare myself to defend my concerns before I even walk into the exam room. It’s like I’m constantly preparing for a debate rather than a medical consultation.

Advocating for myself has become a full-time job, and honestly, it’s exhausting. I have to be so careful about how I phrase things so I don't get labeled as "anxious" or "pushy," which is a really thin line to walk. If I speak up too much, the doctor shuts down and treats me like a nuisance. If I stay quiet, nothing happens, and I’m just left sitting in the woods with my negative tests. 

I’ve had to learn how to speak their language—the "clinical" way—just to get them to listen for thirty seconds. But even then, there’s no proactive support. There’s no "Corie, let’s check in on you." It’s just me, banging on the door, trying to prove that I’m not just being dramatic. It shouldn't be this hard to get someone to care about your reproductive future. It feels like I’m fighting the system just to be treated with basic human decency.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think I imagined that we would be shoulder-to-shoulder in this. I pictured us both being invested in the "project," maybe talking about the numbers together, or him being the one to offer a steady, calm perspective when the monthly tests came back negative. I expected him to be an active teammate—someone who would help me track things, maybe even help with the logistics of appointments, just so the weight of the "preconception" stage didn't feel like it was solely on my shoulders.

The reality has been much lonelier. He's a good man, but he’s very much a "let nature take its course" type of person. When I try to show him my charts or explain the science behind why I’m worried, he sees it as me overcomplicating something that should be natural. He doesn't see the internal struggle; he just sees me getting upset, which makes him want to pull back.

It’s not that he’s unsupportive, but his version of support is just saying, "It’ll happen when it happens." That might be comforting for him, but for me, it feels like he’s invalidating the physical reality I’m tracking. I wanted a partner who would look at the data with me and validate the effort, but instead, I’m navigating the "waiting" part entirely by myself. It’s definitely not the united front I envisioned.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It feels like it’s been that way from day one. Honestly, the entire burden—the physical, the emotional, the logistical—is completely on my shoulders. 

Part of it is just the biological reality; the tracking and the cycle analysis are things that happen *inside* me, so I’m the only one who can truly monitor them. But the gap between me and my partner is huge. He just doesn’t see the need to "prepare" in the way I do. He’s happy to leave it to chance, but I’ve already had children—I know what a body feels like when it’s working, and I know when it’s not. My husband thinks the "stress" of tracking is the problem, but he doesn't realize that the *lack* of answers is what’s causing the stress. 

Then there’s the system. The healthcare provider expects me to be the one to initiate every conversation, to bring the research, and to be the "manager" of my own reproductive health. Nobody is proactive. If I don't initiate the appointment, if I don't force the conversation about what’s happening in my body, then nothing happens at all. 

I think what drives this is the assumption that because I’ve done this before, it should be easy. There’s no acknowledgment that every journey is different, or that a woman’s internal clock is something worth listening to. I’m carrying the weight of the hope, the fear, and the medical research all by myself. It’s an incredibly isolating way to start a family.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't actually experienced a pregnancy loss—and I think that’s exactly why the system is failing me. Because my history is "clean"—three pregnancies, three kids, no losses—the doctors seem to think that everything is perfectly fine. It’s like they have this rigid criteria where they only start caring if you’ve suffered a tragedy first. 

It feels like my "success" in the past has become a cage. I’m expected to just breeze through this fourth one because I’ve done it before, but that doesn't mean my body is in the same place it was years ago. When I bring up my concerns, the underlying message is always, "Well, you haven't had a loss, so what are you complaining about?" 

It’s almost like they’re waiting for me to have a bad outcome before they’ll consider doing a basic workup. I don't *want* to have a loss to "prove" that there’s an issue. I want to be proactive *so that* I don't have to experience one. It’s terrifying that the system seems to prioritize reacting to disaster over preventing it. I feel like I’m standing there asking for a seatbelt, and they’re telling me I don't need one because I haven't crashed yet. It’s a completely backward way to practice medicine.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

I think you might have misunderstood me—I haven't actually experienced a pregnancy loss, and honestly, that’s almost the source of the problem. Because I haven’t had a loss, the doctors act like I have no "reason" to be anxious or to ask for extra help. It’s like, because my previous pregnancies were uncomplicated, they’ve decided I’m not allowed to have any concerns about my current reproductive health.

The irony is that I *want* to be proactive specifically so I *don’t* have to go through the grief of a loss. But the system feels like it’s waiting for a "failure" before they’ll give me the time of day. It’s infuriating. They only want to pay attention when something has already gone wrong. I’m just trying to get ahead of the curve, to make sure everything is functioning as it should at twenty-eight, but instead of being supported for being smart about my health, I’m treated like a nuisance who hasn't "earned" the right to be seen by a specialist. 

It makes me feel like my health and my future pregnancy are only worth their resources if I’m in crisis. I don't want a crisis. I just want a healthy baby, and I want a doctor who understands that wanting to be prepared isn't the same thing as being "high-strung."

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think what’s been weighing on me the most—and maybe it’s the hardest thing to put into words—is the feeling that my humanity is getting lost in the "low-risk" label. It’s like the medical system treats fertility as a checkbox: *Has she had kids? Yes. Any losses? No. Then she’s fine.* 

But my experience isn’t a checkbox. It’s a series of mornings feeling that specific, heavy ache when a test comes up negative. It’s the mental exhaustion of knowing my body better than anyone, but having to swallow my own expertise just to avoid being told I’m "too anxious." I want providers to understand that when a patient comes in with data, it isn’t because she’s trying to be a doctor—it’s because she’s trying to bridge the gap between her reality and their lack of interest.

Living out here in the woods, the isolation is real, but the professional isolation is worse. I just wish there was a way for women like me to be taken seriously *before* the worst happens. I want a maternity care system that sees the person, not just the history on a file. Thank you for listening to me today; it’s honestly the first time in a long time I’ve felt like my experience actually mattered to someone.
